questions.explanation: Any positive number is greater than any negative number, so 9 > -1. A common mistake is seeing the minus sign and treating it like a separator instead of part of the number.
-6 is farther left on the number line than -2, so -6 < -2. A common mistake is choosing -6 because the digit 6 is larger than 2.
Zero is greater than any negative number. That makes 0 greater than both -7 and -1. A common mistake is thinking zero is smaller because it has no sign.
The smallest integer is the one farthest left on the number line. Among these choices, -12 is farthest left. A common mistake is thinking 12 being a larger digit makes -12 greater.

questions.questionText: Choose the true statement about -4 and -10.
options: -4 is greater because it is closer to zero
-10 is greater because 10 is larger than 4
questions.correctAnswer: -4 is greater because it is closer to zero
questions.explanation: Among negative numbers, the one closer to zero is greater. Since -4 is closer to zero than -10, -4 > -10. A common mistake is looking only at the digits.

questions.questionText: Which symbol makes the statement true: -8 ___ 2?
questions.explanation: Any negative number is less than any positive number, so -8 < 2. A common mistake is thinking 8 being a larger digit makes -8 greater.

questions.questionText: A temperature of -3 degrees is compared with 5 degrees. Which statement is correct?
options: Only zero can be compared to temperatures
questions.explanation: A warmer temperature is represented by the greater number. Since 5 > -3, 5 degrees is warmer. A common mistake is focusing on the digit 3 and forgetting the negative sign.
-14 is farther left than -9 on the number line, so it is smaller. A common mistake is treating the larger digit as the greater integer.

questions.questionText: Which number line idea correctly compares -1 and 4?
options: Both are to the left of zero
questions.explanation: Numbers farther right are greater. Since 4 is to the right of -1, 4 > -1. A common mistake is forgetting that positive numbers are always to the right of negative numbers.

questions.questionText: A bank account is at -20 dollars and then rises to -6 dollars. Which balance is greater?
questions.explanation: -6 is closer to zero than -20, so -6 is greater. A common mistake is choosing -20 because 20 is a larger digit.

questions.questionText: A student says -11 > -7 because 11 is greater than 7. What is the mistake?
options: The student ignored that numbers farther left are smaller
The student should always choose the larger digit
The student forgot that -11 is positive
questions.correctAnswer: The student ignored that numbers farther left are smaller
questions.explanation: On the number line, -11 is farther left than -7, so -11 is smaller, not greater. A common mistake is comparing only the digits without thinking about position.
Positive numbers are greater than zero and any negative numbers. That makes 2 the greatest value in the list. A common mistake is choosing 0 because it sits between positive and negative numbers.
-25 is farther left than -18 on the number line, so it is smaller. A common mistake is choosing -25 because 25 is the larger digit.

options: Zero is to the right of every negative number
questions.correctAnswer: Zero is to the right of every negative number
questions.explanation: On the number line, zero is to the right of every negative number, so 0 > -9. A common mistake is treating zero as if it were smaller than all negative numbers.

questions.questionText: An elevator moves from floor 2 down to floor -5. Which floor number is smaller?
questions.explanation: Negative floor numbers are less than positive floor numbers, and -5 is smaller than 2. A common mistake is choosing floor 2 because it is above ground.
-30 is farther left on the number line than -22, so it is smaller. A common mistake is thinking 30 being a larger digit makes -30 greater.
